loneliness.definition: The sad feeling of being alone or not having close people around you.
loneliness.example: Loneliness is a common feeling for new parents.

plagued.definition: Troubled or bothered by something again and again.
plagued.example: The baby might not be the only one plagued with loneliness.

fades.definition: Slowly becomes weaker or less visible.
fades.example: Your old life fades into the background as your new one begins.

established.definition: Set up and working; settled into place.
established.example: Her new life as a mother had yet to get itself established.

geographically.definition: In terms of physical location and distance.
geographically.example: Her family was not geographically close to her.

panicky.definition: Feeling sudden, strong fear and anxiety.
panicky.example: She was shocked, panicky, and lonely after her partner left.

afflicts.definition: Causes pain or suffering to someone.
afflicts.example: Loneliness afflicts many parents, not just single ones.

wits.definition: Mental ability to think clearly; 'at one's wits' end' means unable to cope.
wits.example: She was near to her wits' end with exhaustion.

stigma.definition: A feeling of shame connected to something that society judges negatively.
stigma.example: There is still stigma and shame attached to feeling lonely.

shame.definition: A painful feeling of being wrong or not good enough.
shame.example: Many people feel shame about admitting they are lonely.

alerting.definition: Warning you; drawing your attention to something important.
alerting.example: The feeling of loneliness is alerting you to find company.

isolates.definition: People who are completely alone and separate from others.
isolates.example: Humans are not isolates; we are social animals.

peril.example: Ignore loneliness at your peril, as it can harm your health.

deterioration.definition: The process of getting worse over time.
deterioration.example: Loneliness can cause a deterioration of mental and physical health.

tackling.definition: Trying to deal with or solve a problem.
tackling.example: Tackling loneliness feels like another job when you are tired.

hyper-vigilance.definition: A state of being extremely watchful and alert for danger.
hyper-vigilance.example: Loneliness triggers a state of hyper-vigilance for social rejection.

rejection.definition: Being pushed away or not accepted by other people.
rejection.example: The fear of rejection makes it hard to join new groups.

super-sensitive.definition: Feeling things much more strongly than normal.
super-sensitive.example: Loneliness makes us super-sensitive to signs of being rejected.

coolness.definition: Unfriendly behavior; acting distant or not warm.
coolness.example: She was sensitive to any sign of coolness from other mothers.

rebuffed.definition: Rejected in a rude or unfriendly way.
rebuffed.example: She feared being rebuffed if she tried to make friends.

self-fulfilling.definition: A belief that becomes true because you act in a way that makes it happen.
self-fulfilling.example: Expecting rejection can become a self-fulfilling prophecy.

prophecy.example: Her fear of rejection became a self-fulfilling prophecy.

nosedive.definition: A sudden, steep drop or decline.
nosedive.example: Her confidence took a nosedive after her partner left.

curl.definition: To roll up into a ball shape; to hide away.
curl.example: She wanted to curl up indoors and never come out.

wary.definition: Careful and nervous because you are worried about danger.
wary.example: After being alone, social animals become wary of rejoining a group.

isolated.example: Being isolated makes it harder to trust new people.

rats.definition: Small animals with long tails, often used in scientific studies.
rats.example: Research has shown that even rats feel wary after being separated.

fruit flies.definition: Very small flying insects often used in science experiments.
fruit flies.example: Even fruit flies are cautious about rejoining their group after separation.

override.definition: To take control over something; to choose differently from what instinct says.
override.example: We can use reasoning to override our instincts.

instincts.definition: Natural feelings that make you act in a certain way without thinking.
instincts.example: Your instincts may tell you to hide, but reasoning can override them.

excuses.definition: Reasons you give to avoid doing something.
excuses.example: We come up with all sorts of excuses not to join a group.

inferior.definition: Less good than others; lower in quality or ability.
inferior.example: She thought everyone else was better and she was inferior.

superior.definition: Better than others; higher in quality or importance.
superior.example: Some lonely people tell themselves they are too superior for a group.

weaning.definition: Gradually stopping breastfeeding and giving a baby other food.
weaning.example: She did not want to talk about weaning and nappies with strangers.

nappies.definition: Soft cloth or paper worn by a baby to catch waste (British English for diapers).
nappies.example: Changing nappies is a daily part of caring for a baby.

competent.definition: Having the skill and ability to do something well.
competent.example: Before her baby, she had been a competent professional.

scorn.definition: To look down on something with disrespect; to treat as not good enough.
scorn.example: Isolated people may scorn the idea of social groups.

isolation.definition: The state of being alone and separated from others.
isolation.example: People in isolation are more likely to reject social interaction.

spiral.definition: A pattern that keeps getting worse and worse, going around in circles.
spiral.example: Avoiding people leads to a spiral of withdrawal and more loneliness.

withdrawal.definition: Pulling back from people or activities; becoming less involved.
withdrawal.example: His withdrawal from friends made his loneliness worse.

intensify.definition: To make something stronger or more extreme.
intensify.example: Avoiding people will intensify the sense of social isolation.

combat.definition: To fight against something; to try to stop something bad.
combat.example: She joined a group to combat her loneliness.

breastfeeding.definition: Feeding a baby with milk from the mother's breast.
breastfeeding.example: She joined a breastfeeding-support group for help and friendship.

invaluable.definition: Extremely useful; worth more than can be measured.
invaluable.example: The online support group was invaluable in the middle of the night.

woes.example: Sharing her woes with other parents made them more manageable.

manageable.definition: Possible to deal with; not too difficult.
manageable.example: Talking about problems makes them more manageable.

coping.example: Here is an exercise for coping with loneliness.

recognize.example: Be prepared to recognize when you are feeling lonely.

distrust.definition: A feeling of not being able to trust someone or something.
distrust.example: Loneliness can produce a deep distrust of other people.

inadequate.definition: Not good enough; not having the ability needed.
inadequate.example: New parents often feel inadequate compared to others.

cling.definition: To hold on tightly to something; to not let go.
cling.example: Lonely people may cling to their distrust of others.

reachable.definition: Able to be contacted or approached.
reachable.example: Reach out to others and be reachable yourself.
